item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Three humans, resembling the staff members, burst out of fruits and instead of being kept as instances of SCP-3989-2 observation subjects, they are put back on duty without any question. What? For all they know, they could turn into monsters. That seems oddly unlike the foundation. When SCP-939 gave birth to completely normal infant babies, those babies were killed and only one was left alive, and kept in containment.

Upon second thought, maybe the people were let onto duty because everyone in the facility was infected in some way, by the SCP, to just accept everything that happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (Please delete this comment. I accidentally posted twice)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This shit is good. One complaint though, i would have had a helluva easier time reading this if the subcategories were at the top.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great. Love the world-building and the link to 610. I love everything Sarkic, though, so take that for what its worth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great hidden censors. Absolutely phenomenal how you turned the idea of "what if bone trees tho…" into a very concise ride. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Highlight the block text & footnotes…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of the few SCPs that was genuinely intense for me to read. The pacing, the action, and the freaky horror worked really well for a riveting experience.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the 3000 entries that legitimately gave me nightmares. +1 for making me stay up late on the night of a test.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this SCP a whole lot (I'm a sucker for good, long and detailed Sarkic skips), and it's obvious that a lot of effort went into making it, so an enthusiastic +1 from me! I do have one gripe, though.

MTF Ψ-7 is already in use in quite a number of articles and tales, and is one of the essentially-canon task forces listed at [http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/task-forces]. It is listed as "Home Improvement," a task force specializing "in the undercover investigation, containment, and/or demolition of anomalous buildings or buildings affected by anomalies, particularly residential homes in populated areas." The "Home Improvement" task force, MTF Psi-7, is referenced in over a dozen SCP articles.

So, this is just me being a majorly OCD, nitpicky nerd (the "Fumigators" task force is only mentioned twice in this whole article, after all), but I'm thinking that the alphanumerical designation of the "fumigators" should be changed to something other than Psi-7, preferably to a designation not already listed at the above link, nor at: [http://www.scp-wiki.net/task-forces-complete-list].

It's obviously not really that big a deal, though, and I do love this skip!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a very good SCP. The thing is, the 3rd image reminds me of the crimson from terraria. The red grass, the red trees, and the fleshy looking stone!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First I've read it on mobile, so there were things amiss but second reread was amazing. Absolutely great piece for Sarkicism but… one thing has been bothering me.

yo
– hide block

Kythera. Wasn't SCP-2217 and other writings pinpointing Kythera as being an island somewhere around Greece? I love the reference to that one specific passage but… suddenly Syria? I'm guessing that it's a reference to ya old sarkic dimension-screwing but I'm not sure.
When it comes to blackboxes… When Ion's name was blackboxed I could guess it's him, yeah, must be him, there is enough space for that…. Wait hold on, blackboxes are much wider than that….. Cue run to closest laptop to reveal the rest. As a fun fact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Curses, denied a proper first read due to my mobile ways. This is definitely more enjoyable when you're able to read behind the black boxes, and the whole transition from "fleshy forest place" to "horrifying death blood cult habitat of reincarnation" is smooth and enjoyable. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One thing I noticed—Grant's note for the third log says Ghazalie was infected with 3000-1A. Should that be 3000-V?

Otherwise, this was pretty great. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love myself a good Sarkic article, and this is easily the best new one since I started reading here last June. +1

This really ought to have a few crosslinks though, IMO, as well as another odd reference or two to other Sarkic articles. I'm talking about the sub-designations, with SCP-3000-2A reminding me of the "pale men" from SCP-2191, and maybe the vertical mouth monster if it's present in any other article. I seem to recall it being somewhere, but the specific number has slipped my mind. SCP-610 also ought to be linked to, since you specifically mentioned it. Mostly to improve connectivity between Sarkic articles, which could use more of the wikiwalk potential of older articles.

The subdesignation collapsible ought to appear earlier, I feel. Like at the top of the description page or something similar. It's kind of hard to know what's being talked about without it, and I was continuously confusing one type for another, since there are so many of them. This hinders immersion quite a bit, as I can't be sure whether I should be picturing a 2A or 2B, or which one's even what.

Other than that, the only flaws were some minor spelling mistakes, such as "quazi" instead of the proper "quasi", but those aren't really numerous or glaring enough detract from the article itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nitpick: Having someone who was demoted to D-Class really broke my suspension of disbelief, and threw me out of the story for quite some time. I may be in the minority there because I spent too much time thinking about the logic of how awful it would be as a matter of policy (and of basic safety) to pull something like this. Not gonna stop me from upvoting, but… it's there.

Overall: Wasn't really sold on this until I read the logs. Partially because of that, I'd suggest cutting down on some of the text preceding the logs.

The logs, though… very nice. I love exploration log narratives when done well, and this was done very well. Great story. Very engaging; realistic dialogue; convincing characters. Great payoff.

Enthusiastic upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you want to know more about Sarkic stuff (and if you enjoy body horror then you do) check out the Sarkicism Hub.

item.title: Wow
item.content:encoded: DHMIS

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked it. Gives me the creeps, and reminds me of Dead Space, or The Evil Within, or your generic physical horror games/movies/what have you.

Which is where the flaw comes in for me: I'm not exactly scared. A bit creeped out, maybe, and definitely somewhat grossed out, but the theme of horror is lost on me: I can dig the pure physical horror of the aspect (like headcrabs, facehuggers, the 3000-2s in this one) but any psychological horror I just can't feel. I mean, the only really psychological part was the whole cognitohazard that turned you into crazed fanatics, and the references to Sarkic stuff (which I have no idea what they are), and it just didn't cut it for me.

The only kick I got out of this one would be that I won't be going into an orchards anytime soon - not with anyone who's not familiar with the SCP universe, anyway.

Still, upvote. Solid work is solid work and deserves upvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thoroughly enjoyable. I'm not as well-read as others here, so I haven't been keeping track of other Sarkic stuff, nor can I compare with other entries in the contest because I haven't had time to read anything else yet, but this was a very easy +1 for me, decided long before I was done with it.

Reminds me a lot of "Doom" and "Dead Space" - if that's normal for Sarkic stuff, disregard this but if not, huge props for managing to pull this off. As a big fan of that style, I had a lot of fun with this and especially the interviews and logs. Great dialogue writing.

That said, if I had a gun put to my head and had to mention flaws, I'd say it's pacing is almost perfect for me - "almost" being the key word here. The last log felt like it dragged a bit, but that might just feel like that because the rest is so much better. It builds a slight expectation for un-manned exploration logs that it doesn't deliver on but that didn't bother me - more logs wouldn't add much and would make it too long. The current length is ok. It does have some slightly confusing parts that others already mentioned but none of this detracts from the fun of it. It's horror in that sheer, over-the-top, gate-to-hell sort of way rather than being skincrawly. Loved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I made some minor grammar/tone-wise fixes, revert if wrong. There are still some tone errors in there, run-ons and sentence fragments, stuff. In particular, I don't think the interview transcript should have all-caps. I also think the footnotes should be un-blackboxed.

This is cool. It successfully manages to invoke the feeling of 610's exploration logs, and I had fun reading those. This gets my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice, solid work.

Hoard should be horde, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this kinda hard to follow. Other comments said the opposite, so maybe it's just me. But I couldn't make it to the end, sorry. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bleh. I love Sarkicism, but this is retreading old ground, while doing little to differentiate itself.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love SCP's that show little at first, but then unfold to tell a story.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is spine-crawling

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neat.

The imagery here is both vivid and disturbing, and the final exploration log gave me chills. I'm not the biggest fan of the Sarkic Cult stuff on the site, but this really held my interest from beginning to end.

+1. Solid job FFB.

item.title: Re: errors:
item.content:encoded: The 616 reference is on purpose, was added later after I reread 616 and decided it would work for a different subdesignation. Fixed the three/four thing.

item.title: errors:
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3003-3 table entry still references -616, not -610.

SCP-3000-H table entry says 3 entities, then itemizes 4 of them.

Nice Creepshow. Good Skip, will have to read more entries to decide if it's worthy of -3000.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sarkic/ CoTBG stuff gives me the tingles no matter what I read. This is super fantastic to add to that collection!

But on the hand with the 3000 contest, its not scary enough for me to say for the SCP-3000 spot.

Its well written and engaging, all the little cross links to one of my FAVORITE scips just makes it better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ooh, that's much better. Downvote rescinded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a delightfully paranoid conspiracy horror. I don't think that it is the best entry in the contest, but it definitely is a good contest entry on all metrics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, i do see what you mean about the ending, and have gotten some critique on that aspect before, so that's probably not idiosyncratic.

There's an update at the end now to help out with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did read all the redactions, and I still didn't understand what was going on until I read your comment — it was not at all clear that that's what happened (especially because REAP doesn't usually involve unreaping).

spoilers:
+ show block
– hide block

I still don't understand what's the deal with the scip classification for the entity formerly known as Ghazalie, if he got reborn anyway — are there two of him now?

This still suffers from the problem of having way too much going on for anything to make an impact. It seems to be getting generally positive reviews, so perhaps this is a rather idiosyncratic view of mine, but the ratio of distinct entities to time spent discussing them was very low, and so nothing got developed or understood. combined with the fact that they were only summarized in the last collapsible (and I generally read things in order), this was highly unsatisfying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Go back and highlight those redactions. Also, read other comments. Much of this is covered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is very hard to keep track of because there's so many different things going on (the trees, the larvae, the humanoids, the weird voices), and nothing's really developed enough that I get anything more out of this than "ooo weird bone stuff" — this is much more confusing than horrifying.

Also, this:

Dr. Ghazalie, Echo, India, and Gulf returned to Area-126 on 21/03/2014, having successfully recovered two larval instances of SCP-3000-2A and one instance of SCP-3000-2B. No Foundation equipment or uniforms recovered.

+ show block
– hide block

Entity formerly known as Dr. Farik Ghazalie. Currently uncontained.

These seem to be in direct contradiction to each other. I don't understand what's going on, especially since Echo, India, and Gulf all seemed to die.

Overall, way too confusing. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've missed your presence on the site. This is a pretty great way to return.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or failing that just read it in the page source (Options -> Page Source).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Highlight it and zoom in. Works on andriod. If you don't have an android, copy and paste into a note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there any way to access the blackboxed information on a mobile device, or should I try to get access to something else?

I've enjoyed what I've seen so far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That makes considerably more sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh shit. D: I thought that might be the case, which is why I asked!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You made me seriously afraid of SCP-616 again, and I like the (long awaited) true connection to SCP-610.

This was scary enough on mobile, when I couldn't access the blackboxed stuff and the whole thing had the feel of a nightmare.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An engaging read, but I found myself intrigued instead of scared. Maybe it was because this article felt less involved for the reader compared to other articles in the contest. Or it could just be me, I dunno.

Reserving an upvote post-contest, since this certainly isn't a bad article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for the logs, but you nearly lost me before I got that far in. Your containment procedures are too long and don't build suspense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm incorporated some of the feedback you've provided, thank you. Some of it comes down to colloquial differences in language usage, but the changes I've made should clear it up.

Regarding cognitohazards, however, you're mistaken. Memetic hazards are specific to hazards which are perceived via some form of media, or are otherwise infectious from one mind to another. Cognitohazards can have any vector (visual, informational, auditory, tactile, or chemical), so long as the alterations to thought and perception are anomalous.

And with regards to antimemetics, the on-site material is written in such a way that antimemetics are not widely known or discussed throughout the rest of the Foundation, and this document was most recently updated by Dr. Marshall Grant, former HMCL of SCP-400. He'll necessarily see it through that lens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first image image is set to be displayed as only 300px wide, but is actually 2300px wide. An image that big takes a while to download, even over a reasonable speed connection. You should use an image editing program to shrink the image to 300px wide.

The other images might also need the same treatment.

+ various comments
- various comments

SCP-3000-V refers to an unknown chemical or biological vector responsible for the onset of several cognitohazardous effects in the Area surrounding SCP-3000.

Cognitohazardous effects are induced by seeing/hearing/etc something, not by chemicals or microbes. Perhaps you mean it caused vulnerability to the cognitohazardous effects, such that if you aren't first exposed to the vector then the perception of [whatever] then you aren't affected?

Olea europaea trees within the active zone appear with the same frequency as on the rest of the property, and may outnumber non-anomalous trees present.

Olea europaea trees are non-anomalous. Do you mean SCP-3000-1?

Camera view turns and proceeds through several normal olive trees.

Do you mean "proceeds past several normal olive trees", or literally "through", as in the trees acted like they were holograms?

After approx 15 seconds, a pair of trees at the edge of the visible field appear to stop moving as several more trees manifest through them and move past.

I think that what you're trying to convey here is that, due to the non-Euclidean nature of the space in SCP-3000, some of the trees appear to moving relative to an observer at rest, but the description is ambiguous due to the fact that you're also describing the movement of images across a video screen. That is, the images can be moving across a screen because they're moving relative to the ground, because the camera is moving relative to the ground, because the camera's view is rotating, or some combination of the above. Is a tree moving relative to the ground, with its roots moving through the soil as if the soil was water? If a tree and the soil it's in moving relative to the rest of the ground in the view? Are most of the trees moving across the screen because the camera is changing position, but one of the trees isn't moving across the screen because it's somehow keeping pace with the camera?

Trees that were formerly stationary at the edge of visibility continue moving forward.

Again, its unclear which trees are doing what.

You've been walking through them for the past twenty minutes

Past them, or literally through them?

Vector appears to apply not only to live observation, but also to video and audio recordings.

That sounds like it's antimemetic, not cognitohazardous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am seriously impressed. This is what Sarkicism needed. Despite having written extensively about them, this was still able to disturb me while leaving me eager to delve deeper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I am a Sarkic fan!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You should put in a crosslink to SCP-616 IMO ^.^

This made me fear the Sarkics, which is the first article in a couple of years to do so. Those exploration logs are juicy as fuck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Memeticist redaction. Congratulations, you must have pretty high clearance levels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I must ask what's with this manner of blackboxing. It's rather atypical and the information that are "supposed" to be hidden are ultimately known with relative ease.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3000-2 is an animal in tree's clothing. An ancent animal as old as the curse of flesh.

Also, SCP-610. Jesus that was a rookie mistake. I decided to use 616 for something else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahhh.

And yes, no problems there. :)

What's with all the links to 616 though? That does not seem like the kind of place where trees, anomalous or otherwise, can grow…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Spoilers
– hide block

Not unharmed. Reborn. Remade in the image of War. 3000-7 was able to hide the glory of their new forms. There was an alternate way I could have played it, but the ending as it came out was enough.

You found all the redaction, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last exploration log is some top-quality stuff. Like, Red Sea Object vibes. The very last line is a great zinger, too, though I'll admit a certain amount of confusion as to the precise details of what happened here. I mean, they all come back, alive and unharmed from that last exploration? -V was covering up what really happened or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very comprehensive from an in-universe article composition standpoint, which is a result really hard to pull off, in my opinion. It's disturbing, too, and I love the exploration logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you all for your help in refining this. It feels really good to write a skip again.

All images are sourced from wikimedia commons. Originals can be found here, here, and here.

Hope you all enjoy. Go. Reap.
